sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Hodgkin lymphoma is an uncommon malignancy of B-cell origin with two peaks of incidence: ages 15–30 years and adults aged ≥55 years [1]. The classic presentation involves painless lymphadenopathy, most commonly in the cervical, supraclavicular, or mediastinal regions. Patients may present with B symptoms including unexplained fevers >38°C, drenching night sweats, or weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months of diagnosis. Associated symptoms include alcohol intolerance, pruritus, fatigue, and impaired performance status. CHL accounts for 95% of Hodgkin lymphoma in Western countries, while NLPHL accounts for approximately 5% [3]. CHL is characterized by Reed-Sternberg cells in an inflammatory background, whereas NLPHL is characterized by lymphocytic-histiocytic (LP or "popcorn") cells and lacks Reed-Sternberg cells [1,3]. The majority of patients with CHL present with early-stage disease, but a significant proportion present with advanced-stage disease involving lymph node regions on both sides of the diaphragm.

symptoms.name: B symptoms
symptoms.detail: Unexplained fevers >38°C, drenching night sweats, or weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months prior to diagnosis
symptoms.frequency: Common in advanced-stage disease; presence defines B substage (stage IB, IIB, IIIB, IIIB)

symptoms.name: Alcohol intolerance
symptoms.detail: Pain or discomfort at sites of lymphoma involvement triggered by alcohol consumption
symptoms.frequency: Less common but relatively specific to Hodgkin lymphoma

symptoms.name: Pruritus
symptoms.detail: Generalized or localized pruritus that may be severe and debilitating
symptoms.frequency: Reported in a proportion of patients at diagnosis

symptoms.name: Night sweats
symptoms.detail: Drenching sweats that may require changing bedding, distinct from mild nocturnal perspiration
symptoms.frequency: Common B symptom; defines B substage

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unexplained weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months of diagnosis
symptoms.frequency: Common B symptom; defines B substage

symptoms.name: Fever
symptoms.detail: Unexplained fever >38°C not attributable to infection
symptoms.frequency: Common B symptom; defines B substage

symptoms.name: Painless lymphadenopathy
symptoms.detail: Most commonly cervical, supraclavicular, or mediastinal; may be firm, non-tender, and matted

symptoms.name: Mediastinal mass
symptoms.detail: May be detected on chest radiograph; bulky mediastinal disease defined as MMR >0.33 or MTR >0.35
symptoms.frequency: Present in a significant proportion of early-stage patients

symptoms.name: Hepatosplenomegaly
symptoms.detail: Enlargement of liver and/or spleen on physical examination or imaging
symptoms.frequency: Less common at presentation; splenic involvement is an E-lesion for staging

symptoms.name: Splenomegaly
symptoms.detail: Splenic involvement classified as E-lesion or stage IIIS depending on pattern
symptoms.frequency: Present in a subset of patients

alarms.name: B symptoms
alarms.detail: Unexplained fevers >38°C, drenching night sweats, or weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months prior to diagnosis

alarms.name: Night sweats
alarms.detail: Drenching sweats that may require changing bedding, distinct from mild nocturnal perspiration

alarms.name: Weight loss
alarms.detail: Unexplained weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months of diagnosis

alarms.name: Fever
alarms.detail: Unexplained fever >38°C not attributable to infection
